pageTitle.value: Rolex Awards Video - Listening to the Earth

pageCaption.value: Scientist Michel André’s concern for marine life led to the development of an underwater network of microphones. Discover the video on rolex.org.

previewTitle.value: Ocean noise rings alarm bells - Video

text.value: Michel André, a 2002 Rolex Awards Laureate, has just completed successful first-stage trials of the first-ever whale anti-collision system. But, he says, his research raises a greater issue – the pollution of the world’s oceans with human noise.

pageCaption.value: Tapping into the latest trends in information and telecommunications technology, Jacob Colker has combined volunteering, the internet and mobile phones to pioneer a new form of activism in which almost anyone with a smartphone can devote spare minutes — waiting for the bus or to see the doctor — to a useful charitable or scientific task.

metadescription.value: Tapping into the latest trends in information and telecommunications technology, Jacob Colker has combined volunteering, the internet and mobile phones to pioneer a new form of activism in which almost anyone with a smartphone can devote spare minutes — waiting for the bus or to see the doctor — to a useful charitable or scientific task.

pageTitle.value: Connecting the world with signs - Video

pageCaption.value: The world’s 70 million hearing-impaired people use 126 different languages, each with its own grammar and vocabulary. However, there are very few dictionaries to bridge the gaps between these languages. A Japanese entrepreneur, Junto Ohki, has created SLinto, an online sign-language dictionary that crowdsources signs –which are input with a special keyboard – to help deaf communities across the world converse.

pageTitle.value: Electronic ears to listen to the rainforests - Video

pageCaption.value: Technologist Topher White is giving scientists and conservationists a unique view of wildlife in the world’s rainforests – through an alert system he originally developed to detect illegal logging using old mobile phones.

pageTitle.value: The life-saving mangroves of Manzanar - Video

pageCaption.value: Driven by a long-standing desire for justice, American biologist Gordon Sato is spending his retirement helping some of the world’s poorest people, in Eritrea, to help themselves. His innovative Manzanar project harnesses two of the Eritrean coast’s most abundant resources — intense sunlight and seawater — to grow mangrove plants that can be used not only to feed animals, but also to provide a habitat for fish and shellfish.

WaPageTitle.value: The life-saving mangroves of Manzanar - Video

His innovative Manzanar project harnesses two of the Eritrean coast’s most abundant resources — intense sunlight and seawater — to grow mangrove plants that can be used not only to feed animals, but also to provide a habitat for fish and shellfish.

previewTitle.value: The life-saving mangroves of Manzanar - Video

pageCaption.value: Electrical engineer Dave Irvine-Halliday realised that a single 0.1 watt, white-light emitting diode supplies enough light for a child to read by. The simple but revolutionary technology supplied to homes by his Light Up The World Foundation can light an entire rural village with less energy than that used by a single, conventional, 100 watt light bulb.

pageCaption.value: Lindy Rodwell has devoted the past 11 years to preserving the cranes of Africa, whose magical presence is woven into her childhood memories and the cultural fabric of her native South Africa.
